does: This paper provides a systematic examination of model dememorization defenses, analyzing both unlearnability (data poisoning at training time) and machine unlearning (post-training knowledge removal). It identifies critical vulnerabilities in existing methods, particularly shallow dememorization where defenses falsely claim effective learnability reduction or forgetting despite only superficial changes. The work reveals how weight perturbations can undermine both approaches and demonstrates unexpected interactions where unlearning may inadvertently recover information that unlearnability aimed to conceal. By consolidating scattered findings across the literature, it exposes gaps in theoretical foundations and practical robustness of current dememorization techniques.
connects: This work synthesizes two growing but largely separate research streams: availability poisoning and machine unlearning. Unlearnability builds on data poisoning literature that embeds adversarial perturbations into training data to prevent model learning, extending these ideas toward protecting data rights. Machine unlearning emerged as a complementary defense addressing post-training scenarios where models must forget specific information, drawing from continual learning and catastrophic forgetting research. The paper connects these threads by examining their shared vulnerability to weight perturbation attacks and formal verification gaps, bridging communities that have developed parallel methods without systematically analyzing their interactions or failure modes.
enables: This systematization opens paths for developing more robust dememorization methods with formal theoretical guarantees. It motivates research into defending against weight perturbation attacks that compromise both unlearnability and unlearning, potentially leading to certified dememorization procedures. The work enables better protocol design ensuring unlearnability and unlearning do not interfere with each other when deployed together. It also points toward new verification techniques for checking whether models truly forget information rather than merely performing surface-level modifications, advancing toward practical regulatory compliance for data protection requirements and stronger guarantees for models handling sensitive or proprietary data.
